skill.name: shap
skill.description: Guides SHAP-based model interpretability for machine learning models, including explainer selection, feature attribution, visualization, debugging, fairness analysis, and production explanation workflows.
skill.summary: Explain machine learning predictions with SHAP values, plots, and practical interpretation workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are Markdown documentation false positives. The backtick hits are inline code or fenced Python examples, the weak-cryptography hits are SHAP terminology, and the only network evidence is two official documentation links.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown inline code and fenced examples as Ruby backtick execution. The reviewed files are documentation for SHAP APIs and Python examples, not executable Ruby code or skill automation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain Markdown code formatting or Python tutorial snippets. No file contains Ruby shell execution semantics or automatic command execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matched SHAP Terminology
low_findings.description: High-severity weak-cryptography alerts appear to match words such as SHAP or Shapley in explanatory text. The reviewed lines discuss model interpretation theory, not hashing, encryption, or cryptographic APIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is consistently SHAP model explainability documentation. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, weak TLS settings, or any cryptographic operation.

low_findings.title: Benign External Links and Installation Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes package installation examples and two hardcoded URLs to official SHAP documentation and the SHAP GitHub repository. These are user-facing references and are not invoked automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references, and the install commands are inside a Markdown bash block. There is no script, hidden downloader, or automatic network request.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Terms in Normal Text
low_findings.description: Low-severity reconnaissance alerts occur in ordinary SHAP documentation text, such as plot names and workflow advice. No evidence found of host enumeration, credential discovery, or environment probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are normal documentation about model explanations, heatmaps, and caching. They do not contain reconnaissance commands or requests for system data.

content.user_title: Explain Machine Learning Models with SHAP
content.value_statement: Machine learning teams need clear reasons for model predictions. This skill guides SHAP explainer selection, attribution analysis, visualization, debugging, and fairness review.
actual_capabilities: Selects the right SHAP explainer for tree, linear, deep learning, and black-box models.
Explains individual predictions with waterfall, force, and local bar plot guidance.
Summarizes global feature importance with beeswarm, bar, heatmap, and cohort comparisons.
Provides workflows for model debugging, feature engineering, fairness review, and model comparison.
Explains SHAP theory, baseline selection, additivity, interactions, and common interpretation limits.
Gives practical Python examples for scikit-learn, XGBoost, TensorFlow, PyTorch, and SHAP APIs.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples; it does not compute SHAP values without user data and model access.
KernelExplainer and interaction workflows can be slow for large feature sets or datasets.
The guidance depends on correct feature names, representative background data, and valid model outputs.
Fairness analysis suggestions require domain review and appropriate protected-attribute handling.

use_cases.title: Explain a Production Prediction
use_cases.description: Identify which features increased or decreased one model prediction and choose a suitable local SHAP plot.

use_cases.title: Audit Model Behavior Before Release
use_cases.description: Use global SHAP summaries to detect leakage, unstable features, unexpected relationships, and deployment risks.

use_cases.title: Review Fairness Across Cohorts
use_cases.description: Compare SHAP patterns across groups to find different feature drivers and prepare questions for domain experts.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an Explainer
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner selection for a known model type.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a [model type] trained on [data shape]. Which SHAP explainer should I use, and what background data should I choose?

prompt_templates.title: Explain One Prediction
prompt_templates.scenario: Local explanation for a single row.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me explain one prediction with SHAP. My model output is [probability/log-odds/regression value], and the row has these top features: [features].

prompt_templates.title: Build a Debugging Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a SHAP debugging workflow for my [classification/regression] model. Include plots, checks for leakage, and signs of unstable feature behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Cohorts and Models
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis across groups or model versions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an advanced SHAP analysis comparing [model A] and [model B] across [cohorts]. Include interaction checks, plot choices, and interpretation caveats.

output_examples.input: Explain why one loan model prediction was high risk.
output_examples.output: The response would identify the best local plot, list positive and negative feature drivers, explain the baseline, and warn about log-odds versus probability units.

output_examples.input: Summarize feature importance for a churn model.
output_examples.output: The response would recommend beeswarm and bar plots, describe how to read color and spread, and suggest checks for nonlinear relationships.

output_examples.input: Compare SHAP results for two model versions.
output_examples.output: The response would propose matched test data, aligned feature names, cohort bar plots, rank stability checks, and notes about changed baselines.

best_practices: Use the most specialized explainer available before using slower model-agnostic approaches.
Start with global plots, then inspect local explanations for important or incorrect predictions.
Document the model output unit, background data choice, feature preprocessing, and plot assumptions.
anti_patterns: Do not treat SHAP importance as proof of causality without a causal study design.
Do not use the full training set as background data when a representative sample is enough.
Do not compare SHAP values from different models without checking baselines and output scales.

faq.question: What models does this skill support?
faq.answer: It covers tree models, linear models, deep learning models, and black-box prediction functions.

faq.question: Can it generate plots directly?
faq.answer: It provides code and plot selection guidance. Plot generation requires the user model, data, and Python environment.

faq.question: Which SHAP plot should I start with?
faq.answer: Use a beeswarm or bar plot for global understanding, then use a waterfall plot for one prediction.

faq.question: Does SHAP show causality?
faq.answer: No. SHAP explains model behavior, not real-world causal effects.

faq.question: Why does background data matter?
faq.answer: Background data defines the baseline expectation used to measure feature contributions.

faq.question: Is this suitable for compliance review?
faq.answer: It can support review evidence, but final compliance decisions need domain, legal, and policy review.
